longTailKeywords: Achilles tendon rupture surgery vs conservative

h1Title: Achilles Tendon Rupture: Symptoms, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Achilles tendon rupture is a complete or near-complete tear of the largest and strongest tendon in the body, located approximately 2-6 cm above the heel bone (calcaneal insertion). The injury affects an estimated 18-37 per 100,000 people annually in Western countries, with incidence rising over the past 30 years parallel to growth in recreational sport participation. Middle-aged men aged 30-50 engaging in recreational sport account for the majority of cases — the so-called 'weekend warrior' pattern. The rupture produces an immediate sharp pain, an audible pop, and inability to stand on tiptoe. Both surgical repair and non-surgical functional rehabilitation achieve equivalent rerupture rates of approximately 3-5% when managed with modern accelerated protocols, fundamentally changing management away from automatic surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 18-37 per 100,000 per year in Western countries (Huttunen et al., Scand J Med Sci Sports 2014); incidence tripled from 1980 to 2011
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 3:1; peak incidence in men aged 30-50 (recreational sport); fluoroquinolone antibiotic and corticosteroid use increase risk at any age
keyStats.avgAge: Most common age of rupture 35-45 years in men; women rupture a decade later on average (45-55 years)
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 250,000 Achilles tendon ruptures occur annually in the United States

definition: Achilles tendon rupture (ICD-10: S86.0) is a complete or partial disruption of the calcaneal tendon, formed by the combined tendons of the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles of the posterior calf. The tendon insertion is at the posterior surface of the calcaneal tuberosity; ruptures occur predominantly at the watershed zone of poor vascularity 2-6 cm proximal to the insertion, where the tendon twists as the gastrocnemius and soleus fibers converge. The injury mechanism is most commonly an indirect, eccentric overload during push-off — jumping, pivoting, or sudden acceleration from rest. Pre-existing tendon degeneration (tendinopathy) is present histologically in over 90% of ruptured tendons, indicating that acute rupture is almost always superimposed on chronic structural tendon change. Ruptures are classified as complete (no tendon continuity) or partial (some fibers intact), and as acute (under 4 weeks from injury), subacute (4-12 weeks), or chronic (over 12 weeks). The diagnosis is clinical in most cases, confirmed by the positive Thompson squeeze test and a palpable gap in the tendon; MRI or ultrasound is used when clinical diagnosis is uncertain or to plan operative approach. Management by orthopedic surgeons, sports medicine physicians, and physiotherapists is individualized based on patient age, activity demands, gap size, and time to presentation.

typesClassification.description: Full disruption of all tendon fibers within 4 weeks of injury. Thompson test is positive. Gap is palpable 2-6 cm above the calcaneus. This is the most common clinical presentation requiring a formal management decision between surgery and functional bracing.

Incomplete tear with some tendon continuity. Thompson test may be equivocal or negative. MRI or ultrasound distinguishes partial from complete rupture. Most managed non-operatively with functional bracing and rehabilitation.

Rupture presenting more than 12 weeks after injury, often after initial misdiagnosis. The tendon gap fills with scar tissue and lengthens; functional push-off is lost. Requires surgical reconstruction, typically with a tendon graft (flexor hallucis longus, peroneus brevis, or synthetic scaffold).

typesClassification.type: Avulsion fracture of the calcaneal tuberosity
typesClassification.description: Bony avulsion rather than tendon substance rupture, more common in older patients with osteoporosis or in children. Visible on plain radiograph. Treatment parallels surgical options for tendon rupture with the additional consideration of bone fixation.

primarySymptoms: Sudden severe pain at the back of the lower leg during a push-off movement (jumping, sprinting, pivoting), often described as feeling like being hit or kicked from behind.
An audible or felt 'pop' or 'snap' at the moment of rupture — reported by 50-75% of patients and nearly pathognomonic for complete rupture.
Immediate inability to push off or stand on tiptoe on the affected leg after the acute event.
Palpable gap in the tendon 2-6 cm above the heel, detectable on physical examination in the first hours before swelling obscures it.
Swelling, bruising, and tenderness over the posterior ankle and heel developing over the first 12-24 hours.
Positive Thompson (Simmond's) test: squeezing the calf muscle with the patient prone fails to produce plantarflexion — the most reliable clinical diagnostic sign.
Weak or absent plantarflexion strength: the patient cannot rise on tiptoe on the affected side but retains some (reduced) active plantarflexion through the toe flexors and peroneals.
Antalgic gait with a flattened foot progression angle and a shortened stride on the affected side — the patient walks with the foot flat rather than pushing through the toes.
earlyWarningSigns: Prodromal Achilles tendinopathy with posterior heel pain, morning stiffness, and tendon thickening — present in 60-80% of patients with rupture in retrospect, though most do not seek attention before the acute event.

Previous short-course fluoroquinolone antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin): these inhibit tenocyte function and matrix metalloproteinase regulation, increasing rupture risk 4-fold within 6 months of a prescription.
Prior intratendinous corticosteroid injection to the Achilles tendon — associated with local tendon degeneration and significantly elevated rupture risk.
Sudden worsening of pre-existing Achilles tendinopathy pain during athletic activity without prior trauma should prompt ultrasound assessment to exclude partial rupture.
emergencySigns: Complete loss of plantarflexion with a palpable gap after an acute sports injury — requires same-day orthopedic assessment to determine whether surgical or non-surgical management should begin within the first 48-72 hours, as healing tissue quality deteriorates with delay.
Rapid spreading redness, warmth, fever, and severe pain after Achilles repair or bracing — suggest wound infection or septic tenosynovitis requiring urgent surgical washout.
Progressive foot drop or inability to dorsiflex with posterior heel pain — consider peroneal nerve or posterior tibial tendon injury rather than isolated Achilles rupture.

causes.description: The most common mechanism: sudden dorsiflexion of the ankle against a contracting gastrocnemius-soleus complex during jumping, sprinting, or pivoting. This generates tensile forces exceeding 12× body weight in the tendon, overwhelming the structural integrity of a pre-degenerate tendon. Weekend warriors returning to sport after inactivity are particularly vulnerable because muscle conditioning lags behind the load demand.

Histological analysis of ruptured Achilles tendons reveals mucoid degeneration, lipoid infiltration, and disorganized collagen fibril architecture (tendinopathy) in over 90% of specimens. The hypovascular watershed zone 2-6 cm above the calcaneal insertion is the site of maximal degeneration and rupture. Degeneration does not reliably cause pain, meaning many patients have no pre-rupture warning.

Ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, and other fluoroquinolones chelate magnesium ions and inhibit matrix metalloproteinase regulation in tenocytes, disrupting tendon matrix maintenance. A prescription within the preceding 6 months is associated with a 3.8-4.0-fold increased Achilles rupture risk in large pharmacoepidemiological studies.

Systemic corticosteroids reduce collagen synthesis and impair tendon mechanical properties. Intratendinous injection — which should be avoided in the Achilles — causes focal tendon necrosis and dramatically increases local rupture risk. Peri-tendinous injection, by contrast, carries lower risk.

Laceration, sharp impact, or forced passive dorsiflexion (e.g., falling with the foot fixed in plantarflexion) can rupture an otherwise normal tendon. Direct trauma accounts for fewer than 5% of Achilles ruptures in Western civilian populations.

riskFactors.description: Men rupture the Achilles 3-fold more often than women and at a younger average age. Testosterone-related differences in collagen synthesis and the higher intensity of male recreational sport participation both contribute.

Court and field sports requiring sudden acceleration, jumping, and direction change account for over 60% of non-traumatic ruptures. Risk is highest in athletes who train sporadically rather than consistently.

3.8-4-fold elevated rupture risk during and after fluoroquinolone courses. Alternative antibiotics should be considered for at-risk patients (older age, corticosteroid use, high-demand athletes). FDA black-box warning added in 2008.
Current or recent corticosteroid therapy (any route) impairs collagen synthesis and is associated with elevated tendon rupture risk. Reduce exposure to the minimum effective dose where possible.

Elevated mechanical load on the Achilles tendon combined with adipokine-driven low-grade tendon inflammation increases the risk of both tendinopathy and rupture. Weight management reduces tendon load and injury risk.

Clinically or ultrasonographically detected tendinopathy represents pre-existing structural degeneration. Loading progressed through structured rehabilitation reduces progression to rupture compared with rest or passive management.

Excessive foot pronation and limited ankle dorsiflexion increase eccentric load on the Achilles. Corrective orthotics and flexibility training targeting calf and Achilles length reduce biomechanical risk.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 30-50 engaged in recreational court and field sports constitute the largest group; the UK National Tendon Registry reported 70% of acute ruptures occur in this demographic.
Women tend to rupture the Achilles a decade later (45-55 years), often with a higher proportion of non-sport-related mechanisms including falls or stepping awkwardly.
Elite athletes represent a small proportion of overall cases but have the highest rerupture rates and the greatest recovery stakes, often requiring return-to-sport guidance at 9-12 months.
Patients on chronic fluoroquinolone or corticosteroid therapy are at elevated risk regardless of age or sport participation level.
diagnosisOverview: Achilles tendon rupture is primarily a clinical diagnosis. The Thompson (Simmonds') squeeze test is the gold-standard clinical maneuver: with the patient prone and the knee bent at 90°, squeezing the calf at its widest point normally produces passive plantarflexion; absence of plantarflexion indicates rupture (sensitivity 96%, specificity 93%). A palpable gap 2-6 cm above the calcaneus is present in most acute ruptures before swelling obscures it. The Matles test — patient lies prone with knees flexed to 90°; the affected foot rests in relative dorsiflexion compared to the unaffected side — is a sensitive secondary clinical test. Plain radiographs are performed routinely to exclude calcaneal avulsion fracture, identify the Kager triangle fat pad (which obscures with tendon swelling), and look for tendon calcification. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality when clinical diagnosis is uncertain: it identifies complete versus partial rupture, the gap size, the degree of apposition with plantarflexion, and concurrent pathology. MRI is reserved for equivocal cases, planning surgical reconstruction of chronic rupture, and preoperative assessment of gap length and tissue quality. The Society of Achilles Tendon Ultra-Sound (SATURN) protocol provides standardized sonographic grading. Diagnosis of neglected (chronic) rupture requires MRI to assess gap size, scar tissue quality, and whether augmentation surgery is needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary clinical test to confirm complete Achilles rupture — absence of plantarflexion confirms rupture (sensitivity 96%, specificity 93%)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies face down on the examination table with the knee bent. The examiner squeezes the widest part of the calf muscle and watches for ankle movement. No injection or equipment needed; takes under one minute.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiograph of the ankle (lateral view)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes calcaneal avulsion fracture; assesses Kager triangle fat pad for soft tissue swelling; identifies pre-existing tendon calcification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard two-view ankle X-ray, under five minutes. Soft tissue swelling obliterating the sharp outline of the Kager triangle is an indirect sign of Achilles pathology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging — confirms complete vs partial tear, measures gap, and assesses fiber apposition with passive plantarflexion (determines if functional bracing can bring tendon ends into contact)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the posterior ankle; a linear-array probe is moved over the Achilles while the foot is held in different positions. Takes 10-20 minutes. Dynamic assessment during plantarflexion is essential for non-operative management planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes tendon substance, measures gap length, identifies concurrent pathology; essential for chronic or neglected rupture planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies on the scanner table for 25-35 minutes. No contrast required for tendon assessment. Coronal and sagittal T1 and T2 sequences show fiber disruption, edema, and gap dimensions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Secondary clinical test — relative dorsiflexion of the affected foot at 90° knee flexion compared to the uninjured side confirms reduced resting plantarflexion tension from rupture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clinical examination, no equipment. Takes under one minute. Sensitivity approximately 90%. Used alongside Thompson test to increase diagnostic confidence.

treatmentOverview: Management of acute complete Achilles tendon rupture has evolved substantially: the 2019 UK National Orthopaedic Association (BOA) and 2022 American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society (AOFAS) guidelines endorse both operative and non-operative pathways as equivalent for most patients when functional rehabilitation is started within the first 48-72 hours. The pivotal finding driving this shift is that accelerated functional bracing — early weight-bearing in a boot with a heel raise, commenced within 48 hours — produces rerupture rates of 3-5%, equivalent to modern open or percutaneous surgical repair (rerupture rate 1-4%) without the surgical complication risks (wound infection 2-5%, sural nerve injury 5-10%). Non-operative functional rehabilitation is the preferred approach for the majority of patients, particularly those over 60, with diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, or on immunosuppression, where surgical wound complications are unacceptable. Operative repair is preferred in high-demand athletes, in gaps greater than 5 cm that cannot be approximated with plantarflexion, in young active patients willing to accept surgical risk for marginally earlier return to sport, and when the gap cannot be apposed under ultrasound with the foot in plantarflexion. The standard operative approach for acute rupture is open end-to-end Kessler-type core suture repair (with epitendinous running suture) or minimally invasive (percutaneous) repair. For chronic rupture with significant tendon gap, augmentation procedures using flexor hallucis longus (FHL) tendon transfer, peroneus brevis, or synthetic scaffold are required. Regardless of operative or non-operative route, structured rehabilitation beginning in a functional boot with graduated weight-bearing and plantarflexion exercises is the cornerstone of recovery, progressing to active physiotherapy and sports-specific training at 8-12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Functional bracing (non-operative protocol — boot with heel raise wedges)
medicalTreatments.description: Initial management with the ankle immobilized in 20-30° plantarflexion in a removable boot; early weight-bearing commenced at 48-72 hours; heel wedges progressively reduced over 8-12 weeks. Formal physiotherapy started at week 2 in most protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rerupture rate 3-5% with accelerated weight-bearing protocol (UKSTAR trial 2020); functional outcomes equivalent to surgery at 9 and 24 months

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesic and anti-inflammatory therapy (NSAIDs, paracetamol)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for 5-7 days) or paracetamol for acute pain management. NSAIDs may theoretically impair early tendon healing; reserve for functional impairment rather than routine prophylactic use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction by 30-50% versus placebo for musculoskeletal injury; long-term outcomes not affected by short-course use

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection (adjunct, off-label)
medicalTreatments.description: PRP injected under ultrasound guidance to the repair site to deliver growth factors promoting collagen synthesis. Evidence from RCTs is mixed; no consistent benefit on rerupture rate or functional outcome in pooled analyses. May reduce time to early strength recovery in some trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No significant improvement in ATRS scores at 12 months in largest meta-analysis (Gross et al., AJSM 2020); not currently recommended in major guidelines

surgicalOptions.name: Open end-to-end repair (Kessler or modified Krackow core suture)
surgicalOptions.description: Standard open surgical repair through a posteromedial or posterior incision, approximating tendon ends with locking core sutures and a running epitendinous suture. Weight-bearing in a boot is commenced early (day 2-5) in accelerated post-operative protocols.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Rerupture rate 1-4% with accelerated post-operative rehabilitation; equivalent 12-month functional outcomes to non-operative management in UKSTAR trial; sural nerve injury risk 5-10%

surgicalOptions.name: Minimally invasive (percutaneous) repair
surgicalOptions.description: Tendon repair through small stab incisions using specialized jig devices or suture-passing instruments, avoiding a long wound. Reduces wound complication risk compared with open repair. Sural nerve injury rate historically 10-15% with early techniques but reduced to 1-3% with new instrumentation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent rerupture rate (1-3%) to open repair; faster return to work; reduced infection rate (0.5-1% vs 2-5% for open)

surgicalOptions.name: FHL tendon transfer for chronic or neglected rupture
surgicalOptions.description: When tendon gap exceeds 5-6 cm or the tissue is unsuitable for primary repair, the flexor hallucis longus tendon is harvested through the same posterior incision and secured to the calcaneus, bridging the gap. FHL is preferred for its strength and proximity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Over 80% of patients achieve ATRS scores above 80 (good function) at 12 months; hallux interphalangeal flexion is lost but clinically unimportant in most patients

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (LIPUS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-invasive device applied to the repair site for 20 minutes daily during the early healing phase; proposed to stimulate tenocyte collagen production. Evidence limited to small studies; not currently recommended in NICE or AOFAS guidelines.

whySeeSpecialist: All suspected complete Achilles tendon ruptures should be assessed by an orthopedic surgeon or sports medicine physician within 24-48 hours of injury. Delay beyond 4 weeks substantially worsens outcomes, converting a manageable acute tear into a technically complex chronic reconstruction. A physiotherapist with experience in tendon rehabilitation should be involved from week 1 to direct the functional bracing and graduated weight-bearing program.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an orthopedic surgeon or sports medicine physician with subspecialty interest in foot and ankle surgery and access to musculoskeletal ultrasound for both diagnosis and follow-up of tendon apposition. For athletes, select a center with sports-specific return-to-sport rehabilitation protocols and documented outcomes data. For chronic or neglected ruptures, seek a surgeon experienced in tendon reconstruction and transfer procedures.
hospitalCriteria: Orthopedic foot and ankle surgery service with same-day or next-day acute assessment capability
Musculoskeletal ultrasound available for diagnosis and dynamic apposition testing
Access to functional bracing (boot and heel wedge provision) and early physiotherapy
Physiotherapy service experienced in accelerated Achilles rupture rehabilitation protocols
keyFacilities: Orthopedic foot and ankle surgery centers
Sports medicine clinics with musculoskeletal ultrasound
High-volume physiotherapy practices specializing in tendon rehabilitation
Academic orthopedic centers with tendon reconstruction expertise for chronic cases
preventionStrategies: Progress Achilles loading gradually in sport: use the 10% per week rule for mileage or training intensity increases to avoid overloading a degenerate tendon.
Address Achilles tendinopathy symptoms promptly — eccentric heel-drop exercises (Alfredson protocol) for symptomatic tendinopathy reduce structural degeneration and rupture risk.
Avoid intratendinous corticosteroid injections to the Achilles; peri-tendinous injection by an experienced clinician carries lower risk when anti-inflammatory effect is needed.
Report any new Achilles pain when taking fluoroquinolone antibiotics immediately and discontinue if tendon pain develops — the FDA label carries a black-box warning.
Maintain calf muscle strength and flexibility: regular gastrocnemius and soleus stretching (three times daily, hold 30 seconds) and progressive calf strengthening reduce tendon load during sport.
Warm up and cool down systematically before and after court and field sports; sudden cold-muscle eccentric loading is a precipitant of rupture.
lifestyleModifications: After rupture, protect the repaired tendon from passive dorsiflexion stretching until week 8-10: use the heel-raise wedge provided in the boot at all times.
Comply with the weight-bearing schedule precisely: premature full weight-bearing and delayed weight-bearing are both associated with worse outcomes.
Attend all physiotherapy sessions — structured rehabilitation drives the quality of tendon healing and determines return-to-sport timing more than the surgical or non-surgical choice.
Avoid NSAIDs beyond the first 7 days of injury: prolonged NSAID use may theoretically impair tendon matrix remodeling in the healing phase.
Progress swimming and cycling (non-impact loading) at 6-8 weeks to maintain cardiovascular fitness while the tendon heals.

Do not return to running or pivoting sports without single-leg calf raise strength equal to the uninjured side — typically 12-20 calf raises on a straight leg without fatigue, achieved at 16-24 weeks.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.5-2 g/kg/day) to support tendon collagen synthesis during healing, particularly in the first 12 weeks post-injury
Vitamin C supplementation (500-1,000 mg/day) as a cofactor for collagen crosslinking; evidence supports benefit in the first 6-8 weeks of tendon healing in controlled trials
Collagen peptide supplementation (15 g/day taken 1 hour before rehabilitation exercise) may stimulate tendon collagen synthesis; one RCT showed increased collagen biomarkers with supplementation
Calcium and vitamin D to support overall musculoskeletal health and reduce bone loss during immobilization
avoid: Alcohol excess, which impairs collagen synthesis, prolongs inflammation, and interacts with analgesics
Tobacco and nicotine, which reduce tendon blood flow and impair collagen cross-linking — associated with longer time to tendon healing in cohort studies
Excessive anti-inflammatory supplementation (high-dose omega-3, curcumin) in the first 48-72 hours: the initial inflammatory phase is necessary for healing initiation

exerciseGuidelines: Week 1-2: no weight-bearing initially, then progressive weight-bearing in boot as tolerated following protocol. Week 3-8: full weight-bearing in boot, plantarflexion and dorsiflexion range-of-motion exercises, swimming with a pull buoy (no kick). Week 8-12: wean from boot to shoe with heel raise; begin two-legged calf raises, cycling, aqua running. Week 12-20: progress to single-leg calf raises, jogging on flat ground, then progressive sports-specific loading. Week 20-36: sport-specific training, plyometrics, return to team sport when single-leg calf endurance equals the uninjured side.
dailyManagement: Wear the boot at all times including at night for the first 6 weeks unless specifically directed by your clinician; removing it at night increases rerupture risk.
Elevate the leg above heart level for the first 3-5 days to minimize swelling; use a recliner or pillow support.
Perform the prescribed range-of-motion exercises within the pain-free range at least twice daily from week 2; do not force dorsiflexion beyond the prescribed limit.
Monitor for signs of deep vein thrombosis — calf swelling, warmth, and pain disproportionate to the expected injury — and report these promptly; DVT risk is elevated after lower leg immobilization.
Progress weight-bearing with crutches as directed: most protocols prescribe 50% weight-bearing with crutches at week 1-2, full weight-bearing by week 4.
prognosis: With appropriate management — either functional bracing or surgical repair — the majority of patients achieve good to excellent outcomes. Rerupture rates are equivalent at 3-5% for both accelerated non-operative and modern operative pathways. Patient-reported functional outcomes measured by the Achilles Tendon Total Rupture Score (ATRS) are statistically equivalent between non-operative and operative groups at 9 and 24 months in the landmark UKSTAR trial. Return to recreational sport occurs at approximately 6-9 months for non-elite athletes with consistent rehabilitation. Return to competitive elite sport may take 9-12 months; peak performance recovery at the elite level can require 18-24 months. Permanent functional deficits — residual calf weakness, reduced tendon stiffness, and elevated contralateral injury risk — are present in a subset of patients at 2 years. Re-rupture carries a substantially worse prognosis, with only 60-70% of patients achieving pre-injury activity levels after reconstruction surgery. The best predictor of good outcome is rehabilitation adherence and achieving single-leg calf raise symmetry before sport return.
recoveryTimeline: Weeks 1-6: immobilization in functional boot, progressive weight-bearing. Weeks 6-12: wean from boot, begin calf strengthening. Weeks 12-20: jogging and progressive loading. Weeks 20-32: return to recreational sport. Weeks 32-52: return to full competitive sport for elite athletes. Tendon strength continues improving for 18-24 months post-injury.
complications: Rerupture in 3-5% of both operative and non-operative cases within 2 years — risk is highest in the first 6 months; any sharp pain and pop during rehabilitation must be urgently assessed with ultrasound.
Wound infection (superficial or deep) after open repair in 2-5% of patients — risk elevated in diabetes, obesity, and peripheral vascular disease; percutaneous repair reduces this risk.
Sural nerve injury (numbness or neuroma on the lateral heel) in 5-10% after open repair and 1-3% after minimally invasive repair — typically recovers over 3-6 months.
Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — risk elevated by calf immobilization; graduated compression stockings and pharmacological thromboprophylaxis in high-risk patients are recommended.

Tendon elongation and reduced functional push-off — a stretched healing tendon produces plantarflexion weakness and reduced running performance; heel-rise symmetry testing at return to sport identifies this issue.

Prolonged disability and return-to-sport failure — a minority (10-15%) of patients fail to return to their pre-injury activity level; risk factors include poor rehabilitation adherence, early rerupture, and chronic neglected rupture.

supportResources.name: American Orthopaedic Foot and Ankle Society (AOFAS)
supportResources.description: Patient-focused overview of Achilles tendon rupture, treatment options, and recovery from the leading foot and ankle surgery society.

supportResources.name: Versus Arthritis — Achilles Tendon Problems
supportResources.description: UK-based patient resource covering tendinopathy and rupture, with physiotherapy guidance and support links.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary: Achilles Tendon Disorders
supportResources.description: UK NICE evidence-based guidance for clinicians and patients on assessment and management of Achilles conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achilles tendinopathy (tendinitis) is a chronic overuse condition causing posterior heel pain and tendon thickening but preserved plantarflexion strength and a negative Thompson test. Rupture causes acute sudden pain, an audible pop, positive Thompson test, and inability to push off. Ultrasound or MRI distinguishes the two definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hamstring strain causes posterior thigh pain during sprinting or quick acceleration; the Thompson test is negative, calf squeeze produces normal plantarflexion, and the lesion is proximal in the thigh rather than posterior ankle. Palpation localizes tenderness to the ischium or posterior thigh, not the Achilles tendon.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meniscus Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Meniscus tears present with knee-joint-line tenderness, swelling, and mechanical symptoms (locking, clicking) rather than posterior ankle pain and loss of plantarflexion. McMurray and Thessaly tests are positive for meniscal pathology; Thompson test is negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: ACL Tear
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ACL rupture causes knee instability and a giving-way sensation with anterior knee laxity on the Lachman and anterior drawer tests. Achilles rupture causes isolated posterior ankle weakness without knee-joint laxity. Both may cause an audible pop and acute swelling after a sports injury.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic tendinopathy is present histologically in over 90% of ruptured tendons; managing tendinopathy may reduce rupture risk.

relatedConditions.name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major tendon injury sharing principles of eccentric exercise rehabilitation, progressive loading, and the non-operative vs surgical management debate.

relatedConditions.name: ACL Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: Major sports injury in the differential for acute athletic events; both cause sudden onset with an audible pop and require structured rehabilitation for return to sport.

relatedConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
relatedConditions.relevance: Common posterior-chain sports injury in the differential for posterior limb pain after sudden acceleration; distinguished by location and Thompson test.

faqs.question: What is an Achilles tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: An Achilles tendon rupture is a complete or partial tear of the large tendon connecting the calf muscles to the heel bone. It occurs most often during sports requiring sudden push-off movements such as basketball, tennis, or sprinting. The injury typically causes an immediate sharp pain, an audible pop, and inability to push off on the affected foot.

faqs.question: What does an Achilles tendon rupture feel like?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe a sudden sharp pain at the back of the ankle as if they were struck or kicked by another person. About 50-75% hear or feel an audible pop at the moment of rupture. After the initial pain, walking becomes difficult because the calf muscle can no longer transmit power through the heel effectively.

faqs.question: How is Achilles tendon rupture diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is primarily clinical using the Thompson squeeze test: the examiner squeezes the calf and a complete rupture fails to produce ankle plantarflexion (96% sensitive). A palpable gap 2-6 cm above the heel supports the diagnosis. Ultrasound or MRI confirms complete versus partial rupture and measures the gap size.

faqs.question: Does Achilles tendon rupture require surgery?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Large trials including the 2020 UKSTAR trial showed equivalent rerupture rates (3-5%) and functional outcomes between accelerated non-operative treatment (functional boot with early weight-bearing) and surgical repair. Surgery is preferred in high-demand athletes, large gaps, and young active patients; non-operative treatment is preferred in older patients or those with diabetes or vascular disease.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from an Achilles tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to recreational sport at 6-9 months. Return to full weight-bearing in a boot occurs by week 4-6; the boot is weaned off by week 8-12. Single-leg calf-raise symmetry — the key milestone before sport return — is typically achieved at 16-24 weeks. Elite athletes may need 9-12 months or longer.

faqs.question: What is the Thompson test for Achilles rupture?
faqs.answer: The Thompson (Simmonds') test is performed with the patient lying face down and knees bent at 90°. The examiner squeezes the widest part of the calf muscle. In a healthy tendon, this produces passive plantarflexion of the foot. Absence of plantarflexion indicates a complete Achilles rupture. Sensitivity is approximately 96%.

faqs.question: Can antibiotics cause Achilles tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fluoroquinolone antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) carry an FDA black-box warning for tendon rupture. They inhibit tenocyte function and increase Achilles rupture risk approximately 4-fold, particularly in patients over 60 and those on corticosteroids. Report any new tendon pain while taking fluoroquinolones to your doctor immediately.

faqs.question: What causes an Achilles tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is sudden eccentric overload during sport — jumping, pivoting, or sprinting — in a tendon already weakened by degeneration. Pre-existing tendinopathy is present in over 90% of ruptured tendons. Fluoroquinolone antibiotics, systemic or intratendinous corticosteroids, and obesity are major modifiable risk factors.

faqs.question: What is the rerupture rate after Achilles tendon repair?
faqs.answer: With modern accelerated rehabilitation protocols, rerupture rates are 3-5% for non-operative treatment and 1-4% for surgical repair — not statistically different. The old fear that non-operative treatment carries much higher rerupture rates applies only to older cast-immobilization protocols, not modern functional bracing.

faqs.question: What is a partial Achilles tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: A partial rupture means some tendon fibers remain intact. The Thompson test may be equivocal or negative, and the patient retains some (reduced) plantarflexion power. MRI or ultrasound confirms the diagnosis. Most partial ruptures are managed non-operatively with functional bracing and rehabilitation.

faqs.question: Can I walk with a ruptured Achilles tendon?
faqs.answer: Yes, but with difficulty. Most patients can walk with a flat-footed shuffle but cannot push off or stand on tiptoe. Walking should be in a protective boot with heel wedges; unsupported walking risks rerupture before tendon healing is established. Full weight-bearing in the boot is encouraged from 48-72 hours in accelerated protocols.

faqs.question: What are the risks of Achilles tendon surgery?
faqs.answer: Open surgical repair carries a wound infection rate of 2-5%, sural nerve injury (numbness) in 5-10%, and deep vein thrombosis risk from immobilization. Minimally invasive repair reduces wound complications but requires specialist experience to minimize nerve injury. Rerupture after repair occurs in 1-4% of patients with good rehabilitation.

faqs.question: How is Achilles tendon rupture treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Non-operative treatment uses a functional boot with a heel raise starting in 20-30° plantarflexion, with early weight-bearing from 48-72 hours. Heel wedges are progressively removed over 8-12 weeks. Formal physiotherapy begins at week 2. This achieves equivalent outcomes to surgery at 9 and 24 months with lower complication risk.

faqs.question: Does a ruptured Achilles tendon heal on its own?
faqs.answer: Tendons do not regenerate to identical pre-injury tissue even with optimal treatment. Scar tissue bridges the gap, and tendon properties differ from normal. However, functional outcomes with rehabilitation are excellent for most patients. Untreated rupture without immobilization heals in a lengthened position, producing permanent weakness.

faqs.question: When can I return to sport after Achilles tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: Return to recreational sport is typically at 6-9 months and elite sport at 9-12 months. The key milestone is equal single-leg calf-raise strength on both sides — usually 20 pain-free calf raises at full height. Returning before this milestone substantially increases rerupture risk.

symptomKeywords: pop at back of ankle during sport
felt kicked back of leg no one there
metaTitle: Achilles Tendon Rupture: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Achilles tendon rupture: sudden tendon tear causing a pop and inability to push off. Surgery vs functional bracing, recovery timeline, and rehab reviewed.
